topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, cream-colored paper felt alien beneath my fingertips. I’d found it tucked inside a seldom-used antique book, a forgotten volume of obscure poetry. As I unfolded the pages, the words began to coalesce—my name, my childhood address, the precise dates of my life, all meticulously detailed. A cold knot tightened in my stomach. I scanned the narrative, my breath catching in my throat as I realized the voice speaking was undeniably mine, yet the events described felt profoundly skewed, missing crucial emotional context. My hands began to tremble, the paper slipping slightly. Who had done this? I stood up abruptly, knocking over a nearby porcelain figurine, the sound echoing loudly in the quiet study.

[story 2]

It hit me like a physical blow when I read the final paragraph. The smooth, elegant prose detailing my career trajectory, my supposed triumphs, felt like a meticulously constructed façade. I reread the section about my pivotal decision in my twenties, and a flush crept up my neck. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic rhythm I couldn't slow down. I dropped the document onto the mahogany desk, the sound sharp and definitive. I leaned back in my chair, staring blankly at the ceiling, trying to process the sheer audacity of it. Every nerve ending seemed hyper-sensitized; I kept running my fingers through my hair, a gesture of deep, unsettled confusion.

[story 3]

The discrepancy was staggering. The biography claimed I had a lifelong passion for astronomy, an interest I’d never possessed. I traced a line of text with my index finger, the ink seeming to mock my disbelief. A sudden, sharp intake of breath escaped me. My vision seemed to narrow slightly. I pushed the book away, my movements jerky and uncoordinated. I paced the length of the room twice, my footsteps heavy on the hardwood floor. When I stopped by the window, the afternoon sunlight seemed too bright, too harsh. I pressed my palms against my eyes, fighting a rising tide of sheer disorientation, needing to steady myself before I could even begin to formulate a response.
